speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two. The survivor is Susan Brunn. What happened after you were rejected by Elizabeth Arden? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a school, some kind of a beauty school, to learn to become a beautician, mainly, to-- to become a manicurist in hopes of establishing something, to have a job, to have a trade when I emigrate, which was up in the air. But we-- I know we tried to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who paid for your schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who paid for my schooling? Schooling was, at that time, I think, free in Germany. And I think the high school was-- my mother paid for. If there was a charge, I don't know. I think schools were free to a certain point.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in 1938. Could you describe where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We always lived in an apartment. We had an apartment, I think, in the neighborhood of Nurnberger Platz, which was in a very nice neighborhood, the west of Berlin [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on a street called Achenbachstrasse. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had, I think, normal, very nice apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How tall was the building where the apartment was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was maybe a six-story building. We had the ground floor apartment towards the back. We had even a little balcony, which went out into a backyard.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember. One, two, three, I think four and a half. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But my mother had always rented two. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had the half room. And the kitchen didn't count as a room in-- in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the building have indoor plumbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything, every comfort was there-- electricity, plumbing, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any favorite radio programs that you used to listen to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked the American jazz, the-- the-- not the rock, the-- the nice-- like what they played in the '40s, this type of music, shows, from shows, music from shows. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember my mother had an old gramophone with such a big horn and which you had to wind up. And she had certain kind of records which we played. There was no television and all those things. So you kept entertained with that. And you played checkers at night or domino.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were some of your favorite musicians that you would listen to or singers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was very into classical music. And I remember my mother went away for-- for a week or so on vacation with a friend. And she left me money to eat. And I didn't. I bought myself a ticket to-- the-- to the Berliner Philharmonie to hear Claudio Arrau in a Chopin piano recital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From my earliest days on I remember that I had-- I loved classical music, certain classical music.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you went to cosmetic school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember I was employed doing something completely different for about a couple of months, three months, four months, in a brokerage, by-- by a broker. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to do the filing. And it was-- he was already before his emigration. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I knew the job wasn't for a long time. And he knew it wasn't for a long time. It was just to help him get order in all his papers, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then he left, I don't know, three, four, or five months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you make a living after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. I don't know. My mother had to make a little living for both of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the next thing that happened in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next thing that happened in my life was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I was-- oh, I forgot a big part. I went-- I had plans to go to Israel, Palestine at that time. And I went for a year and a quarter to Hachshara.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you ever hear that before? That's right. I forgot that. And I was in a place in Silesia called [INAUDIBLE]. And there I learned to become a farmer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was there for over a year, until the beginning, middle of '39. How did you enjoy that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked it, maybe because of the comradeship. And it was very nice. I enjoyed it. But it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did it end?

speaker: interviewee
text: It ended, because my time was finished. You had to go so and so long to be-- to graduate. Three months in the-- in the cow-- what do you call that? In the-- you learned in the kitchen three months. And three months you took care of the cows. And three months you took care of the chickens or what. I don't know exactly anymore. But that's what it was. You had a certain schedule to learn everything to go into a kibbutz in Israel. But that didn't work out anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it stopped. There were no boats anymore going. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And at a certain point, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the danger for men, they thought, was greater than for women. And on the last few boats, they only took men.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very happy, but I could understand it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not knowing at that time that men and women, there was no difference. They were both in the same danger. But at that time, they thought men are more dangerous. Because at the Crystal Night, they took the men and not the women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a witness to a Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we woke up that morning. And across the street from us was a Jewish tailor shop. And we heard that they banged in the glass windows and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] screamed. And-- I don't know. Somehow we must have been warned. Because a good friend of ours, a man and his son, came to our house. And we were hiding them. Because in our house, we were only women. My mother and I, we were registered. So they didn't come to the house to pick up men. They knew it's only the two of us. And we were hiding two men until the action was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you check out what had happened on the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you could see everything from the window and from-- and then, of course, a radio and the newspapers. Spontaneous action [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it said. But it wasn't a spontaneous action.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother increase her efforts to try to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember she was in touch with a cousin, went to Chile, and tried that he should get her a visa. But nothing worked out. It was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after this?

speaker: interviewee
text: After this, we were both put into forced labor. My mother worked for a company, which was called Deutsche [INAUDIBLE]. And I worked for Siemens. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we lived like this as long as we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for Siemens?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a welder. I was welding little like field telephones.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn how to weld?

speaker: interviewee
text: They taught me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran Siemens?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. This was a big company. And there were different departments and different foremen. And it was a very big place. And there were lots of Jews there working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your supervisor at Siemens?

speaker: interviewee
text: You told me to think about that. And I thought about it. And his name was Herr Gericke. And I see him in front of me running in this grey-- it was a like long robe [INAUDIBLE]. And he happened to be very nice and very compassionate. He saw to it that I get every week, I think, a quarter or a half a liter milk. Because I was inhaling the fumes. And Jews were actually not supposed to get it, but I got it. And he was very nice. And like he would ask me-- we had one day a month we-- we could get a day off for laundry for-- and he asked me if I had that day already. And I said, no. He said, tomorrow you stay home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He tried to be as nice as he was allowed to under the circumstances, as I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a Nazi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Deep down, I don't think he was a Nazi. But he had to belong to the [? party. ?] And he had to wear this little thing here. Everybody had to, more or less. And there were very few who didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the little thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a pin with the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sign of the [? party ?] of the [? SA. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at Siemens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 1942. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In December of 1942, we went underground. And that's when I didn't go to work anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to go underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if you found this out in your interviews. At that point, people got a list still. You will be picked up to go to be relocated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you will be taken, so-and-so. And my mother had, as I told you, always had a room rented. And there was a man living with us in the house. And he got the list and was picked up. And when one of a household was picked up and got the list, we knew that the others would follow very soon. It was always in-- you know, in succession, one after the other. And so we decided we don't want to. We will go underground and try our luck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had the opportunity, because we had quite a few gentile friends. And my uncle, who lived in Berlin, was married to a gentile lady, this Uncle Felix. And my mother went to them for the first few days or weeks. And I went to other friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Meyer. Their name was Meyer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I don't know his-- Ludwig, Ludwig and Teresa Meyer. And he was also Jewish. And she was gentile. And for that reason, they were-- they were not treated right away the way we were. As long as they were-- I think even until the end if they stayed together, they had to go to a working camp or something. But they could live. They were not killed. They were not deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do while you were staying with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I helped them in the house. And I couldn't go out. I was there and helped them. Occasionally, at dark in the evening, I went out for half an hour for a walk, to have some fresh air. We had no ration cards. We had nothing. They had to share what they had with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food like that you were eating at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever you got on stamps, what they got, which was stretched. You got like 2 pounds of meat for the week per person. And you got a half a pound of butter. And you got 5 pounds of potatoes. It was all rationed. Not so much in the beginning, as it got always worse later on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember when we got ration cards, which year that was. We still-- it must have been before. Because we had some before, which were printed with a J, Jew. And we could only get certain things on them. For instance, vegetables we didn't get. We only got one vegetable. And that's why I wouldn't touch it today anymore-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] turnips, the big, yellow turnips. And we had to eat that for years as our vegetable. And my husband loves it. And he wants it. And I'm not going to cook it, so he has to buy it already cooked. And when I heat it up, I heat it up like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet Klaus at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met Klaus very early in '42 or even at the end of '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you in hiding with at that time when you met Klaus?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't in hiding then at all. I met him when I still lived normal in a-- in an apartment with my mother. And this friend, she also, [INAUDIBLE] was living with us at that time. And he was introduced to me from a mutual friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His full name? Klaus Hans Wertheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was special about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I don't know, nothing. Nothing was special about him. But he was a nice, young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the friendship change? What happened to your friendship with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we were very good friends. And when the point came by the end of 1942, he helped me with getting out of the apartment. I remember that he came with a car and took whatever we wanted to take and helped us to-- to put it wherever we wanted to, to my mother's brother. I remember he took a big bag with potatoes-- I see that in front of me-- and our valuables and our clothes. The furniture, everything, we left in the apartment. We only took a few Oriental small carpets, which we all-- all-- things which we hoped we will be able to sell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Klaus Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Klaus had a Jewish father and a gentile mother. And he was bought up Protestant. But he had only Jewish friends and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was against Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he help you go into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? Well, he helped me find, at times, suitable places to stay. He got me this place through his mother with this man who we all called Papi [INAUDIBLE]. That was his name, Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. And we all called him Papi [INAUDIBLE]. And he was like a father to six, seven, eight Jews. I don't know how many anymore he had up there in hiding. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He helped me with food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went to live in his place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after you left the Meyers'?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you very honestly. I went to so many places that I don't remember anymore from where to where. I had a friend Victoria. I don't know where-- she brought me somewhere where she-- where the people left for vacation. And she got the apartment for two weeks. Or-- I was in so many places that I can't even keep track.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many would you estimate that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: 12, 15, 16 different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Over what period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 19-- from December '42 till the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get from one place to another? How did you hear of each place?

speaker: interviewee
text: By telephone or by-- by talking to the people, saying I don't have a place to stay for tomorrow. Can I come to you for a couple of nights? By having lots of gentile friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think the gentile-- German gentiles opened their homes to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were, firstly, friends. And secondly, these were all people who were anti-Nazis and who wanted to help as much as they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you estimate that there was a big group of them in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, I don't know how big the group was. But there were quite a few people who were living underground with somebody like I did. And there must have been quite a number of people who helped at one point or another. There were also some which didn't know, which gave shelter to somebody who wanted to make maybe a little money, who, who, said, like-- I lived with my husband, with Klaus, together in a hotel, where we said, we just got bombed out in Lubeck, I think. And we have to rent a hotel room for two weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't-- oh, yeah, he registered under his name, Mr. and Mrs. He had papers. I didn't. So there were all kinds of people, who-- but I would say 90% of the people who helped wanted to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what your state of mind was at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frightened. At times, I felt I-- I can't go through it anymore. I want to get it over with already. Because I had the feeling I will be caught anyway. But then there was something in me. And I said, I'll try another day and another day. And it worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your mother dealing with this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my mother had more courage at times than I did. Because I see her. She was in a different place. And I was in a different place. And she got from somebody a chicken or something. And she cooked it wherever she was. And she had a pot. And she came to the place where I was, bringing me a piece that I should have something too. So she dealt quite well with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a strong woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you communicate with her to find out where she was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly by telephone. We always were able either to telephone direct or through like this Mrs. Meyer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was like our [? centrally-- ?] we called her, and she gave the message. And my mother called her. And we always knew of each other, where we are. And we met at times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your relationship when-- with-- with Klaus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there wasn't-- it didn't take too long when you live together. You know, we-- we fell in love. And he helped me as much as he could. And we lived together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we was-- suddenly it went also in a certain way against the gentile, [NON-ENGLISH], half-- the half-and-half people. And he was put to-- it was called Organisation Todt. And he was a mechanic. And he was put into a place in Erfurt [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to repair underground certain-- I don't know what it was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put the-- the important places underground, so that they couldn't be bombed. And these people had to go and do the work, the repair and the-- and since he was a mechanic, he was put there. There were people who had no trade, no-- they didn't learn anything. They took them to clear minefields. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's what I heard. I mean, I haven't seen it. And I don't know. But that's what was said. And he got me a room in Erfurt [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a place where he told a cock-and-bull story about who I am and that he is-- everybody knew that he is a [NON-ENGLISH]. Otherwise, he wouldn't have been there and that I'm gentile and the we have-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that we applied for permission to get married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That you could try. You were allowed to do that. But it took usually months and months and even years. And it never came through. But you were at least legal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of papers did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I bought [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] false papers, which I don't even remember. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. When you worked, you had-- like you work here for a defense factory, you had a certain pass. It was a paper like that from a job and with the name of Susan [? Martin. ?] They told me it's always good to take-- if possible, keep the first name and take the second name with the same initial like your real name, so that in case you were to otherwise make a mistake that you don't do it. And as I found out, they were not too good. But they held up a couple of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was once caught on a bus. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not me, I wasn't caught. I mean, there was a check up of everybody. And they accepted my paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll pick up on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
